FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, N=10,246, baseline 2016-2018, year one 2017-2019, year two 2018-2020, ages 9-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures total screen time (hours/day across 6 modalities: TV, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media) and social media use at baseline; problematic social media use (SMAQ) and problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ) at year two

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and eating disorder symptoms at two-year follow-up; cross-sectional associations between problematic screen use and eating disorder symptoms

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports OR with 95% CI: total screen time OR 1.05-1.12 (p<0.001), social media OR 1.18-1.55 (p=0.001-0.039); problematic social media OR 1.26-1.82 (p<0.001); problematic mobile phone OR 1.26-1.82 (p<0.001)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective cohort design with logistic regression examining longitudinal associations between baseline screen time and year-two eating disorder symptoms, plus cross-sectional analyses

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Eating and Weight Disorders, 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports cross-sectional analyses with screen time/problematic use as dependent variable AND includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (anxiety, impulsivity, BMI) AND reports screen time→eating disorder outcome associations. Does not meet FT-EC7 criteria which requires longitudinal-only demographic predictors of screen time use.
notes: This is a strong inclusion. Both longitudinal and cross-sectional components with substantial phone/SM variables. Anxie and impulsivity measured; parent-reported baseline eating disorder symptoms included as confounder.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data, n=8,489 analytic sample from 11,880 total participants recruited from 21 sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is only measured via ABCD Parent Screentime Questionnaire (PSQ) and Youth Screen Time (STQ) as a control/confounder variable in analyses. No smartphone-specific or social media-specific variable is reported separately or with independent analysis. Screen time treated purely as adjustment covariate.

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time is not analyzed as an independent predictor. It appears only as an adjustment covariate in models examining pet ownership → physical activity and sports involvement. No inferential test reported for screen time as predictor of any outcome.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficient, OR, or statistical test reported for screen time associations. Screen time is adjustment variable only.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multiple logistic and linear regression models examining pet ownership effects on parent physical activity, youth involvement in sports, and physical activity levels

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 and FT_IC3 not met. Screen time is adjustment covariate only with no independently reported association. Cannot evaluate FT_EC7 when paper does not meet core inclusion criteria.
notes: EXCLUSION: Paper uses ABCD but screen time is only adjustment variable, not the primary exposure of interest. No smartphone or social media variable analyzed as independent predictor. Per coder prompt E1, covariate without coefficient = FT_IC3 NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data. Participants n=11,876 assessed annually ages 9-12 years as part of ABCD Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen usage types: TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat. Self-reported via questionnaire assessing typical hours per day on each modality. Textxing, online video, social media are specifically phone/SM-related variables.

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal network analysis models estimated to examine connections between screen usage types and PLEs across three time points. Network analysis with edge weights, centrality measures, and statistical tests (NetworkComparison package comparing global strength across time points; p<0.05 for significance)

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports edge strengths (Tables 2-4, ranging 0-1), centrality metrics (expected influence, closeness, betweenness), and bridge indices. E.g., texting centrality, online video centrality reported; network comparisons with statistical tests of differences (M=0.10, p=.029 for T1 vs T3)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal network analysis examining prospective associations between six screen usage types and psychotic-like experience symptoms across three time points in early adolescence

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Comprehensive Psychiatry, 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time/digital media is independent variable predicting psychotic-like experience outcomes (not DV). Network includes individual psychological/clinical outcome (PLEs = mental health symptoms). Does not meet FT-EC7 longitudinal demographic-only criterion.
notes: Strong inclusion. Network analysis methods reported with numeric edge strengths and centrality indices at each time point. Texting and online video identified as highest centrality. Comprehensive examination of screen usage type differences in relation to PLE symptoms over time.
